question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified / cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged borrower who is the maker of a sell offer, after becoming unhealthy, call cancelRoot on their own root to invalidate all pending sell offers in that tree, preventing any taker from filling reduce-only sell offers that would have repaid debt and restored health, thereby indefinitely blocking the only path to voluntary debt reduction while remaining immune to liquidation until a separate liquidator acts? Preconditions: borrower is maker of a sell offer tree signed with EcrecoverRatifier, borrower has debt > maxDebt, no liquidatorGate. Call sequence: (1) borrower signs Merkle tree of sell offers, (2) borrower takes debt via taker filling sell offer, (3) oracle drops, borrower becomes unhealthy, (4) borrower calls cancelRoot(borrower, root) before any liquidator acts, (5) assert liquidate still succeeds and isRootCanceled does not affect liquidate path. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable regardless of ratifier state. Scoped impact: liquidation bypass if cancelRoot could somehow block liquidate (it cannot via the direct path, but test that no indirect path exists). Proof idea: fuzz (root, timing) and assert midnight.liquidate succeeds after cancelRoot; assert debtOf decreases.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: cancelRoot / isAuthorized] Can an authorized operator of an unhealthy borrower call cancelRoot(borrower, root) on EcrecoverRatifier to cancel the borrower's entire signed offer tree, then immediately call midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverRatifier, false, borrower) to revoke the ratifier's authorization, making all future EcrecoverRatifier-based takes for that borrower-as-maker impossible, while the borrower's debt remains and liquidation is unaffected? Preconditions: borrower has authorized operator, borrower is maker of sell offers using EcrecoverRatifier, borrower is unhealthy. Call sequence: (1) operator calls cancelRoot, (2) operator revokes ratifier authorization, (3) assert liquidate(borrower) still succeeds. Invariant tested: liquidation path does not depend on ratifier state; unhealthy positions remain liquidatable. Scoped impact: liquidation bypass if operator actions could freeze liquidation. Proof idea: unit test asserting liquidate succeeds after operator cancels root and revokes ratifier auth.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker forge a valid Merkle proof by exploiting the unordered Merkle tree construction in HashLib.isLeaf, where the leaf index determines left/right position, to pass an offer hash that was never signed by the maker, enabling an unauthorized sell offer fill that creates debt for a victim borrower without their consent, making the borrower unhealthy and liquidatable? Preconditions: attacker controls ratifierData, victim borrower has no debt, market uses EcrecoverRatifier. Call sequence: (1) attacker constructs crafted (root, leafIndex, proof) such that HashLib.isLeaf returns true for a forged offer hash, (2) attacker provides a valid signature over the crafted root, (3) attacker calls take with forged ratifierData, (4) assert borrower.debt increases without borrower consent. Invariant tested: ratifier approval must bind correct maker, market, offer, amount, price, deadline, nonce, salt. Scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation leading to forced liquidation. Proof idea: fuzz (leafIndex, proof[]) to find collisions in isLeaf; differential test against reference Merkle implementation.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker exploit Solidity ecrecover signature malleability (s in upper half of secp256k1 curve, v flipped) to produce a second valid signature (v', r, s') for the same digest that was used to sign a Merkle root, allowing replay of a canceled root's offers by submitting the malleable signature after the maker called cancelRoot? Preconditions: maker signed root R with (v, r, s), maker called cancelRoot(maker, R), attacker computes malleable (v^1, r, n-s). Call sequence: (1) maker signs root R, (2) maker calls cancelRoot(maker, R), (3) attacker submits take with same root R but malleable signature (v^1, r, n-s), (4) assert isRatified reverts with RootCanceled (root R is still canceled regardless of signature). Invariant tested: canceled offers cannot be filled. Scoped impact: liquidation bypass if canceled sell offers could be replayed to create debt. Proof idea: unit test with malleable signature after cancelRoot; assert RootCanceled is thrown.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData with proof.length == 0 (single-leaf tree where root == hashOffer(offer)) and a valid signature over that root, for any offer they choose to construct, effectively self-signing any offer as long as they are the maker or an authorized signer, bypassing the intent that the maker pre-committed to a specific set of offers? Preconditions: attacker is maker or authorized signer, attacker constructs offer with desired parameters (e.g., maxUnits = type(uint256).max, no expiry constraint). Call sequence: (1) attacker constructs offer O with desired debt-creating parameters, (2) attacker computes root = hashOffer(O), (3) attacker signs root with their key, (4) attacker calls take with proof = [], leafIndex = 0, (5) assert take succeeds and creates debt. Invariant tested: ratifier approval must bind correct offer parameters. Scoped impact: excess debt creation enabling bad debt or forced liquidation of counterparty. Proof idea: unit test with empty proof; assert consumed and debt accounting are correct.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified / hashOffer] Can an attacker exploit a second-preimage collision in the Merkle tree where an internal node hash (keccak256(left || right) of two 32-byte values) equals a valid EIP-712 offer hash, allowing the attacker to prove membership of a fake offer in a tree that the maker signed, enabling unauthorized offer fills that create unexpected debt? Preconditions: attacker finds or constructs a Merkle tree where an internal node hash collides with a valid offer hash. Call sequence: (1) maker signs a legitimate tree root, (2) attacker constructs proof treating an internal node as a leaf, (3) attacker calls isRatified with the crafted proof, (4) assert InvalidProof is thrown (EIP-712 offer hashes include typehash prefix making collision computationally infeasible). Invariant tested: Merkle proof must bind to the exact signed offer. Scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation. Proof idea: differential fuzz test comparing isLeaf output against reference implementation with random proof arrays.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid signature over root R for a tree containing offer O1 (a sell offer with small maxUnits) to also ratify offer O2 (a sell offer with large maxUnits) by constructing a Merkle proof that maps O2's hash to a leaf position in the same tree, if O2's hash happens to equal a sibling node in the tree? Preconditions: attacker controls offer parameters, maker signed root R. Call sequence: (1) maker signs tree with O1 and padding offers, (2) attacker constructs O2 with parameters such that hashOffer(O2) equals a sibling node in the tree, (3) attacker submits take for O2 with crafted proof, (4) assert InvalidProof is thrown. Invariant tested: offer hash must be a genuine leaf in the signed tree. Scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill creating excess debt. Proof idea: fuzz offer parameters to find hash collisions with tree nodes.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData with leafIndex having high bits set beyond proof.length (e.g., leafIndex = 2^256 - 1 with proof.length = 1) to cause HashLib.isLeaf to revert with LeafIndexOutOfRange, and if this revert propagates through isRatified and take, can it be used to DoS liquidation-adjacent takes that would repay an unhealthy borrower's debt? Preconditions: taker submits malformed ratifierData, borrower is unhealthy. Call sequence: (1) taker calls take with leafIndex >> proof.length != 0, (2) assert take reverts with InvalidProof (LeafIndexOutOfRange bubbles up), (3) assert this does not affect liquidate path. Invariant tested: malformed ratifier data must not affect liquidation availability. Scoped impact: DoS of debt-reducing takes, indirectly keeping borrower unhealthy. Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex values near 2^proof.length boundary.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData with proof.length > 20 to trigger HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash revert with TreeTooHigh, causing isRatified to revert, and use this to DoS any take that would reduce an unhealthy borrower's debt, while the borrower remains unliquidatable due to liquidationLocked during the take callback? Preconditions: borrower is seller in a take, borrower is unhealthy, attacker controls ratifierData. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits take with proof.length = 21, (2) assert take reverts with TreeTooHigh, (3) assert liquidate is unaffected. Invariant tested: ratifier revert must not block liquidation. Scoped impact: DoS of debt-reducing takes. Proof idea: unit test with proof.length = 21; assert revert propagates correctly.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an authorized operator of a borrower call cancelRoot(borrower, root) during the onLiquidate callback (while the liquidation is in progress, after collateral has been seized but before the loan token is pulled), to cancel a root that the borrower was relying on for a reduce-only sell offer, preventing the borrower from using that offer tree after the liquidation completes? Preconditions: borrower has authorized the liquidator's callback contract, borrower has a signed offer tree, liquidation is in progress. Call sequence: (1) liquidator calls liquidate with callback, (2) onLiquidate callback calls cancelRoot(borrower, root) via isAuthorized check, (3) assert cancelRoot succeeds (isAuthorized is live), (4) assert borrower's offer tree is now canceled. Invariant tested: authorization must not allow unintended state mutations during liquidation callbacks. Scoped impact: griefing borrower's future debt reduction capability. Proof idea: unit test with liquidation callback that calls cancelRoot.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker who is an authorized signer for a maker sign a Merkle root containing a sell offer with maxUnits = type(uint256).max and no expiry, then after the maker revokes the signer's authorization, the attacker cannot use that signature anymore (isAuthorized check is live at take-time), but can the attacker front-run the revocation to fill the offer before authorization is revoked, creating excess debt for the maker? Preconditions: maker authorized signer S, signer S signed root with large sell offer, maker is about to revoke S. Call sequence: (1) maker calls setIsAuthorized(S, false, maker), (2) attacker front-runs with take using S's signature, (3) assert take succeeds if front-run wins, (4) assert maker's debt increases. Invariant tested: authorization revocation must be atomic with respect to offer fills. Scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation leading to unhealthy position. Proof idea: test ordering of setIsAuthorized vs take in same block.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use the same (sig, root, leafIndex, proof) ratifierData to fill the same offer multiple times (replay), since EcrecoverRatifier has no per-fill nonce or consumed tracking, relying entirely on Midnight's consumed mapping to prevent overfill? Preconditions: offer has maxUnits > 0, consumed[maker][group] < maxUnits. Call sequence: (1) taker fills offer partially with valid ratifierData, (2) taker fills same offer again with identical ratifierData, (3) assert consumed[maker][group] increases correctly and does not exceed maxUnits, (4) assert no excess debt is created. Invariant tested: offer fill amount cannot exceed remaining offer capacity. Scoped impact: excess debt creation via offer replay. Proof idea: fuzz (units1, units2) with units1 + units2 > maxUnits and assert ConsumedUnits revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid (sig, root, leafIndex, proof) for offer O in market M1 to ratify the same offer O in a different market M2 (with different collateral params or maturity), since the offer hash includes the full market struct via hashMarket, but if two markets differ only in a field not included in hashMarket (e.g., rcfThreshold), could the same signature ratify offers in both markets? Preconditions: two markets M1 and M2 differ only in rcfThreshold, maker signed offer O for M1. Call sequence: (1) maker signs offer O with market M1, (2) attacker constructs offer O' identical to O but with market M2 (different rcfThreshold), (3) attacker calls take with O' and M1's ratifierData, (4) assert InvalidProof is thrown (hashMarket includes rcfThreshold). Invariant tested: ratifier approval must bind to the exact market. Scoped impact: cross-market offer replay creating unauthorized debt. Proof idea: differential test of hashMarket with varying rcfThreshold.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified / hashOffer] Can an attacker exploit the fact that hashOffer includes keccak256(offer.callbackData) to construct two offers with different callbackData that produce the same offer hash (keccak256 preimage collision), allowing the attacker to use a signature for one offer to ratify a different offer with a malicious callback? Preconditions: attacker finds keccak256 preimage collision (computationally infeasible but worth testing boundary). Call sequence: (1) maker signs offer with benign callbackData, (2) attacker constructs offer with malicious callbackData that hashes to same value, (3) assert isRatified accepts the malicious offer. Invariant tested: offer hash must uniquely identify the offer. Scoped impact: unauthorized callback execution during take. Proof idea: fuzz callbackData values; assert hashOffer is injective for all tested inputs.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker submit ratifierData where the abi.decode of (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[]) succeeds but with a proof array of length 0 and leafIndex = 0, for an offer whose hash equals the root (single-leaf tree), and use this to ratify any offer that the maker signed as a single-leaf root, effectively allowing the maker's signature over any individual offer hash to serve as a valid ratification? Preconditions: maker signed individual offer hashes as roots (common pattern for single-offer trees). Call sequence: (1) maker signs root = hashOffer(O), (2) attacker submits take with proof = [], leafIndex = 0, root = hashOffer(O), (3) assert isRatified succeeds, (4) assert this is the intended behavior and does not allow cross-offer confusion. Invariant tested: single-leaf tree ratification must only ratify the exact signed offer. Scoped impact: unintended offer ratification. Proof idea: unit test single-leaf tree with proof.length = 0.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an attacker who is authorized by a maker (via coarse-grained isAuthorized) call cancelRoot on behalf of the maker to cancel ALL of the maker's signed offer trees simultaneously, then immediately create new debt for the maker by taking a different offer (not using EcrecoverRatifier), leaving the maker with unexpected debt and potentially unhealthy? Preconditions: maker authorized attacker, maker has multiple signed offer trees, maker has collateral. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls cancelRoot(maker, root1), cancelRoot(maker, root2), ..., (2) attacker takes a non-EcrecoverRatifier offer from maker, (3) assert maker's debt increases, (4) assert maker becomes unhealthy. Invariant tested: authorized address must not be able to create unauthorized debt for the authorizer. Scoped impact: unauthorized debt creation via coarse authorization abuse. Proof idea: unit test with authorized attacker canceling roots and taking offers.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData where the Signature struct has v = 0 or v = 1 (non-standard values), causing ecrecover to return address(0), which is then caught by the require(_signer != address(0)) check, but if address(0) were somehow authorized (which the Ratification.spec invariant proves is impossible), could this bypass the signer check? Preconditions: attacker submits v = 0 signature. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls isRatified with v = 0, (2) assert ecrecover returns address(0), (3) assert InvalidSignature is thrown. Invariant tested: address(0) cannot be a valid signer. Scoped impact: unauthorized offer ratification. Proof idea: fuzz v values {0, 1, 2, ..., 255} and assert InvalidSignature for all non-standard v.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified is a view function (no state changes) to call it directly (not via Midnight) and observe whether a given (offer, ratifierData) pair would be accepted, enabling front-running of offer fills or information leakage about pending offers? Preconditions: attacker calls isRatified directly (not via Midnight). Call sequence: (1) attacker calls ecrecoverRatifier.isRatified(offer, ratifierData) directly, (2) assert NotMidnight revert is thrown. Invariant tested: isRatified must only be callable by Midnight. Scoped impact: information leakage enabling front-running of liquidation-adjacent takes. Proof idea: unit test direct call to isRatified; assert NotMidnight.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature for a sell offer with offer.reduceOnly = true to create debt for the maker (seller), then after the maker becomes unhealthy, use the same ratifier data to fill the offer again (if not fully consumed), causing the maker's debt to increase further beyond what the maker intended, making the position more deeply unhealthy and harder to liquidate profitably? Preconditions: maker signed a reduce-only sell offer, maker has existing credit, maker's credit is fully consumed after first fill. Call sequence: (1) taker fills reduce-only sell offer, consuming all maker credit, (2) taker attempts second fill of same offer, (3) assert MakerCreditOrDebtIncreased revert (reduceOnly check), (4) assert debt does not increase beyond credit. Invariant tested: reduce-only offers must not increase maker debt. Scoped impact: excess debt creation. Proof idea: fuzz (units, existing credit) and assert reduceOnly invariant.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature for a sell offer in a market with a liquidatorGate to take the offer (creating debt for the maker/seller), then when the maker becomes unhealthy, the liquidatorGate prevents all liquidators from liquidating, effectively trapping the maker in an unhealthy state? Preconditions: market has liquidatorGate that blocks all liquidators, maker signed sell offer using EcrecoverRatifier. Call sequence: (1) taker fills sell offer, maker becomes unhealthy, (2) liquidator calls liquidate, (3) assert LiquidatorGatedFromLiquidating revert, (4) assert maker remains unhealthy indefinitely. Invariant tested: unhealthy positions must remain liquidatable (gate is a market-level design choice, not a bug). Scoped impact: liquidation bypass via gate + ratifier combination. Proof idea: unit test with blocking liquidatorGate; assert liquidation is blocked by design.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature for a buy offer (lend offer) from a maker, fill it to give the maker credit, then use the maker's coarse authorization to call cancelRoot and setConsumed to manipulate the maker's offer state, preventing the maker from using their credit to repay debt in another market? Preconditions: maker authorized attacker, maker has credit in market A and debt in market B. Call sequence: (1) attacker fills maker's buy offer, giving maker credit, (2) attacker calls cancelRoot(maker, root) to cancel maker's sell offer tree in market B, (3) maker cannot reduce debt in market B via EcrecoverRatifier offers, (4) assert maker's debt in market B remains, (5) assert liquidation in market B is unaffected. Invariant tested: authorization abuse must not block liquidation. Scoped impact: griefing borrower's debt reduction. Proof idea: multi-market unit test with authorized attacker.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker submit a ratifierData with a valid signature but where the offer passed to isRatified (via take) has been modified from the signed offer (e.g., different tick, different maxUnits), causing hashOffer(offer) to differ from the leaf hash in the Merkle tree, and assert that InvalidProof is correctly thrown? Preconditions: attacker has valid (sig, root, leafIndex, proof) for offer O, attacker modifies O to O'. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls take with O' but ratifierData for O, (2) assert hashOffer(O') != hashOffer(O), (3) assert isLeaf returns false, (4) assert InvalidProof is thrown. Invariant tested: ratifier must bind to the exact offer parameters. Scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill with modified terms. Proof idea: fuzz single field mutations of offer and assert InvalidProof.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker exploit the fact that the domainSeparator in EcrecoverRatifier uses block.chainid (not a cached INITIAL_CHAIN_ID like Midnight does) to create a cross-chain replay attack where a signature valid on chain A is also valid on chain B after a hard fork, allowing unauthorized offer fills on the forked chain that create debt for the maker? Preconditions: hard fork changes block.chainid, maker signed offers on original chain. Call sequence: (1) maker signs root on chain A (chainId = 1), (2) hard fork creates chain B (chainId = 2), (3) attacker submits take on chain B with chain A signature, (4) assert domainSeparator differs (block.chainid = 2 on chain B), (5) assert InvalidSignature or Unauthorized is thrown. Invariant tested: signatures must not be valid across chains. Scoped impact: cross-chain unauthorized debt creation. Proof idea: unit test with vm.chainId manipulation.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature for a sell offer with offer.expiry = type(uint256).max and offer.start = 0 (no time constraints) to fill the offer at any time in the future, including after the market's maturity, creating debt for the maker post-maturity (which should be blocked by CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity in take)? Preconditions: maker signed sell offer with no expiry, market has passed maturity. Call sequence: (1) warp to block.timestamp > market.maturity, (2) taker calls take with valid ratifierData, (3) assert CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity revert (sellerDebtIncrease > 0 post-maturity). Invariant tested: debt must not increase after maturity. Scoped impact: post-maturity debt creation. Proof idea: fuzz timestamp around maturity boundary; assert debt invariant.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Critical Liquidation bypass or incorrect liquidation that lets unhealthy borrowers avoid liquidation or lets liquidators seize excess collateral'] [Function: isRatified] Can an attacker use a valid EcrecoverRatifier signature for a sell offer with offer.group = bytes32(0) (default group) to fill the offer, then call setConsumed(bytes32(0), type(uint256).max, maker) (if authorized) to cancel all offers in the default group, preventing the maker from using any other offers in that group, while the maker's debt from the filled offer remains? Preconditions: attacker is authorized by maker, maker has multiple sell offers in group bytes32(0). Call sequence: (1) attacker fills one sell offer, (2) attacker calls setConsumed(bytes32(0), type(uint256).max, maker), (3) assert all other offers in group bytes32(0) are now unconsumable, (4) assert maker's debt remains. Invariant tested: authorized address must not be able to cancel offers in a way that traps debt. Scoped impact: griefing maker's debt reduction. Proof idea: unit test with authorized setConsumed after partial fill.
